Shearwater 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • importing-and-preparing-gps-data
    • Movement Metrics
    • seabird_fisheries_overlap_workflow
    • Individual, Population, and Conservation Summaries
    • Trip Segmentation and Movement Behavior

Movement Metrics

Source: vignettes/movement-metrics.Rmd
movement-metrics.Rmd
library(Shearwater)
library(dplyr)
library(geosphere)
library(sf)

Overview

This vignette demonstrates how to calculate trip-level movement metrics from a segmented Wedge-tailed Shearwater GPS dataset. The goal is to move from raw GPS fixes to biologically useful movement summaries, such as trip distance, trip duration, total path length, maximum distance from the colony, track centroids, and foraging range.

The functions covered in this vignette are:

calc_trip_distance()
calc_trip_duration()
calc_path_length()
calc_max_distance_from_colony()
calc_track_centroid()
calc_foraging_range()

These functions are useful after GPS points have been cleaned, standardized, and assigned to trips.

Load example GPS spreadsheet data

For this vignette, we use the example GPS spreadsheet saved in the package as:

inst/extdata/example_wtsh_gps.csv

The raw spreadsheet has columns such as Date, Time, Latitude, Longitude, Altitude, Speed, Course, Type, Distance, and Essential.

gps_path <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "example_wtsh_data.csv",
  package = "Shearwater",
  mustWork = TRUE
)

gps_raw <- read.csv(
  gps_path,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
head(gps_raw)
##        Date     Time Latitude Longitude Altitude Speed Course Type Distance
## 1 8/15/2013 20:01:59 21.57396 -158.2765    99.20     0      0   -2     0.00
## 2 8/15/2013 20:05:33 21.57431 -158.2762    -4.37  9144    146    0    46.38
## 3 8/15/2013 20:09:26 21.57412 -158.2764    38.35  1584    291    0    27.01
## 4 8/15/2013 20:12:32 21.57435 -158.2762    -6.62     0     60    0    34.85
## 5 8/15/2013 20:15:38 21.57437 -158.2760   -24.37     0    333    0    17.49
## 6 8/15/2013 20:18:43 21.57406 -158.2765   -50.77     0     80    0    57.53
##   Essential
## 1         1
## 2         1
## 3         1
## 4         1
## 5         1
## 6         1

Standardize the GPS data

The movement metric functions expect standard column names such as:

  • track_id
  • datetime_gmt
  • longitude
  • latitude
  • at_colony
  • trip_id

The raw spreadsheet stores date and time separately, so we first combine them into one timestamp column.

gps <- gps_raw %>%
  mutate(
    track_id = "bird_001",
    datetime_gmt = as.POSIXct(
      paste(Date, Time),
      format = "%m/%d/%Y %H:%M:%S",
      tz = "UTC"
    ),
    longitude = Longitude,
    latitude = Latitude
  ) %>%
  arrange(track_id, datetime_gmt)

head(gps)
##        Date     Time Latitude Longitude Altitude Speed Course Type Distance
## 1 8/15/2013 20:01:59 21.57396 -158.2765    99.20     0      0   -2     0.00
## 2 8/15/2013 20:05:33 21.57431 -158.2762    -4.37  9144    146    0    46.38
## 3 8/15/2013 20:09:26 21.57412 -158.2764    38.35  1584    291    0    27.01
## 4 8/15/2013 20:12:32 21.57435 -158.2762    -6.62     0     60    0    34.85
## 5 8/15/2013 20:15:38 21.57437 -158.2760   -24.37     0    333    0    17.49
## 6 8/15/2013 20:18:43 21.57406 -158.2765   -50.77     0     80    0    57.53
##   Essential track_id        datetime_gmt longitude latitude
## 1         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:01:59 -158.2765 21.57396
## 2         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:05:33 -158.2762 21.57431
## 3         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:09:26 -158.2764 21.57412
## 4         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:12:32 -158.2762 21.57435
## 5         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:15:38 -158.2760 21.57437
## 6         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:18:43 -158.2765 21.57406

Define the colony location

For this example, we treat the first GPS point as the colony location. In a real analysis, the colony coordinates should come from field metadata or a known colony reference point.

colony_coords <- c(
  lon = gps$longitude[1],
  lat = gps$latitude[1]
)

colony_coords
##        lon        lat 
## -158.27649   21.57396

Next, we calculate each point’s distance from the colony.

gps <- gps %>%
  mutate(
    dist_to_colony_m = geosphere::distHaversine(
      cbind(longitude, latitude),
      matrix(
        c(colony_coords["lon"], colony_coords["lat"]),
        nrow = n(),
        ncol = 2,
        byrow = TRUE
      )
    )
  )

summary(gps$dist_to_colony_m)
##      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
##      0.00     15.89  11624.95  24404.11  52758.65 105602.76

Create an at-colony flag

Trip segmentation depends on knowing whether each GPS point is at the colony or away from the colony. Here, points within 75 meters of the colony are labeled as TRUE, while points farther away are labeled as FALSE.

gps <- gps %>%
  mutate(
    at_colony = dist_to_colony_m <= 75
  )

table(gps$at_colony)
## 
## FALSE  TRUE 
##   795   674

The exact distance threshold should be chosen based on GPS accuracy, colony size, and the biological question. For this example dataset, 75 meters creates a simple distinction between colony and away-from-colony points.

Segment trips

The movement metric functions require a trip_id column. Rows where the bird is at the colony receive NA, while away-from-colony points are assigned a numbered trip.

segmented <- segment_trips(
  gps,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  datetime_col = "datetime_gmt",
  colony_flag_col = "at_colony",
  trip_id_col = "trip_id"
)

head(segmented)
## # A tibble: 6 × 17
##   Date   Time  Latitude Longitude Altitude Speed Course  Type Distance Essential
##   <chr>  <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> <int>  <int> <int>    <dbl>     <int>
## 1 8/15/… 20:0…     21.6     -158.    99.2      0      0    -2      0           1
## 2 8/15/… 20:0…     21.6     -158.    -4.37  9144    146     0     46.4         1
## 3 8/15/… 20:0…     21.6     -158.    38.4   1584    291     0     27.0         1
## 4 8/15/… 20:1…     21.6     -158.    -6.62     0     60     0     34.8         1
## 5 8/15/… 20:1…     21.6     -158.   -24.4      0    333     0     17.5         1
## 6 8/15/… 20:1…     21.6     -158.   -50.8      0     80     0     57.5         1
## # ℹ 7 more variables: track_id <chr>, datetime_gmt <dttm>, longitude <dbl>,
## #   latitude <dbl>, dist_to_colony_m <dbl>, at_colony <lgl>, trip_id <int>

Check how many points were assigned to each trip.

segmented %>%
  count(track_id, trip_id)
## # A tibble: 14 × 3
##    track_id trip_id     n
##    <chr>      <int> <int>
##  1 bird_001       1     1
##  2 bird_001       2     1
##  3 bird_001       3     1
##  4 bird_001       4     1
##  5 bird_001       5     1
##  6 bird_001       6     1
##  7 bird_001       7     1
##  8 bird_001       8     1
##  9 bird_001       9     2
## 10 bird_001      10     6
## 11 bird_001      11   648
## 12 bird_001      12     1
## 13 bird_001      13   130
## 14 bird_001      NA   674

Add a simple foraging label

The calc_foraging_range() function can estimate the spatial area used for foraging. To demonstrate this, we create a simple behavior label using speed.

In this example, points away from the colony with relatively low speed are labeled as "foraging", while other away-from-colony points are labeled as "commuting". Colony points are labeled as "colony".

segmented <- segmented %>%
  mutate(
    phase = case_when(
      is.na(trip_id) ~ "colony",
      Speed <= median(Speed, na.rm = TRUE) ~ "foraging",
      TRUE ~ "commuting"
    )
  )

table(segmented$phase)
## 
##    colony commuting  foraging 
##       674       617       178

This is only a simple demonstration. In a full analysis, behavior classification should be based on a better movement model or a validated rule-based method.

Calculate straight-line trip distance

The calc_trip_distance() function calculates the straight-line distance from the first point of a trip to the last point of that same trip.

trip_distance <- calc_trip_distance(
  trip_data = segmented,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  trip_id_col = "trip_id",
  datetime_col = "datetime_gmt",
  lon_col = "longitude",
  lat_col = "latitude",
  output_col = "trip_distance_m"
)

trip_distance
## # A tibble: 13 × 8
##    track_id trip_id start_lon start_lat end_lon end_lat n_points trip_distance_m
##    <chr>      <int>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>    <int>           <dbl>
##  1 bird_001       1     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  2 bird_001       2     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  3 bird_001       3     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  4 bird_001       4     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  5 bird_001       5     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  6 bird_001       6     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  7 bird_001       7     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  8 bird_001       8     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
##  9 bird_001       9     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        2            58.5
## 10 bird_001      10     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        6            68.2
## 11 bird_001      11     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6      648           890. 
## 12 bird_001      12     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        1             0  
## 13 bird_001      13     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.3      130         55957.

This metric is useful for understanding the displacement between the start and end of a trip. It does not measure the full route traveled by the bird.

Calculate trip duration

The calc_trip_duration() function calculates how long each trip lasted. It uses the first and last timestamp within each trip.

trip_duration <- calc_trip_duration(
  trip_data = segmented,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  trip_id_col = "trip_id",
  datetime_col = "datetime_gmt",
  units = "hours",
  output_col = "trip_duration"
)

trip_duration
## # A tibble: 13 × 6
##    track_id trip_id trip_start          trip_end            n_points
##    <chr>      <int> <dttm>              <dttm>                 <int>
##  1 bird_001       1 2013-08-15 20:31:05 2013-08-15 20:31:05        1
##  2 bird_001       2 2013-08-15 20:43:37 2013-08-15 20:43:37        1
##  3 bird_001       3 2013-08-15 21:38:53 2013-08-15 21:38:53        1
##  4 bird_001       4 2013-08-15 22:04:57 2013-08-15 22:04:57        1
##  5 bird_001       5 2013-08-16 20:40:02 2013-08-16 20:40:02        1
##  6 bird_001       6 2013-08-16 22:48:48 2013-08-16 22:48:48        1
##  7 bird_001       7 2013-08-17 00:38:30 2013-08-17 00:38:30        1
##  8 bird_001       8 2013-08-17 02:49:04 2013-08-17 02:49:04        1
##  9 bird_001       9 2013-08-17 03:30:01 2013-08-17 03:33:06        2
## 10 bird_001      10 2013-08-17 03:48:31 2013-08-17 04:04:16        6
## 11 bird_001      11 2013-08-17 05:38:35 2013-08-18 20:21:50      648
## 12 bird_001      12 2013-08-18 21:58:08 2013-08-18 21:58:08        1
## 13 bird_001      13 2013-08-18 23:13:22 2013-08-19 06:45:45      130
## # ℹ 1 more variable: trip_duration <dbl>

The output gives one row per trip with the start time, end time, number of GPS fixes, and duration.

Calculate total path length

The calc_path_length() function calculates the total distance traveled along the GPS path. Unlike straight-line trip distance, path length sums the distance between consecutive GPS points.

path_length <- calc_path_length(
  track_data = segmented,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  trip_id_col = "trip_id",
  datetime_col = "datetime_gmt",
  lon_col = "longitude",
  lat_col = "latitude",
  output_col = "path_length_m"
)

path_length
## # A tibble: 13 × 4
##    track_id trip_id path_length_m n_points
##    <chr>      <int>         <dbl>    <int>
##  1 bird_001       1           0          1
##  2 bird_001       2           0          1
##  3 bird_001       3           0          1
##  4 bird_001       4           0          1
##  5 bird_001       5           0          1
##  6 bird_001       6           0          1
##  7 bird_001       7           0          1
##  8 bird_001       8           0          1
##  9 bird_001       9          58.5        2
## 10 bird_001      10          71.2        6
## 11 bird_001      11      551986.       648
## 12 bird_001      12           0          1
## 13 bird_001      13       66507.       130

This metric is usually larger than straight-line trip distance because it follows the bird’s observed route.

Calculate maximum distance from colony

The calc_max_distance_from_colony() function finds the farthest point reached from the colony during each trip.

max_distance <- calc_max_distance_from_colony(
  trip_data = segmented,
  colony_coords = colony_coords,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  trip_id_col = "trip_id",
  lon_col = "longitude",
  lat_col = "latitude",
  output_col = "max_distance_from_colony_m"
)

max_distance
## # A tibble: 13 × 4
##    track_id trip_id max_distance_from_colony_m n_points
##    <chr>      <int>                      <dbl>    <int>
##  1 bird_001       1                       87.1        1
##  2 bird_001       2                       75.5        1
##  3 bird_001       3                       94.3        1
##  4 bird_001       4                      121.         1
##  5 bird_001       5                       79.6        1
##  6 bird_001       6                       83.4        1
##  7 bird_001       7                       76.6        1
##  8 bird_001       8                      118.         1
##  9 bird_001       9                      136.         2
## 10 bird_001      10                      123.         6
## 11 bird_001      11                   105603.       648
## 12 bird_001      12                       79.5        1
## 13 bird_001      13                    56147.       130

This metric is useful for understanding how far birds travel from the breeding colony.

Combine trip-level movement metrics

The separate metric tables can be joined into one trip-level summary table.

trip_metrics <- trip_distance %>%
  left_join(
    trip_duration,
    by = c("track_id", "trip_id")
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    path_length,
    by = c("track_id", "trip_id")
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    max_distance,
    by = c("track_id", "trip_id")
  )

trip_metrics
## # A tibble: 13 × 16
##    track_id trip_id start_lon start_lat end_lon end_lat n_points.x
##    <chr>      <int>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>      <int>
##  1 bird_001       1     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  2 bird_001       2     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  3 bird_001       3     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  4 bird_001       4     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  5 bird_001       5     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  6 bird_001       6     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  7 bird_001       7     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  8 bird_001       8     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
##  9 bird_001       9     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          2
## 10 bird_001      10     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          6
## 11 bird_001      11     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6        648
## 12 bird_001      12     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.6          1
## 13 bird_001      13     -158.      21.6   -158.    21.3        130
## # ℹ 9 more variables: trip_distance_m <dbl>, trip_start <dttm>,
## #   trip_end <dttm>, n_points.y <int>, trip_duration <dbl>,
## #   path_length_m <dbl>, n_points.x.x <int>, max_distance_from_colony_m <dbl>,
## #   n_points.y.y <int>

This table is the main trip-level movement summary. Each row represents one trip, with columns for distance, duration, path length, and maximum distance from the colony.

Calculate track centroid

The calc_track_centroid() function calculates the spatial center of a track or set of points. This can be useful for quickly summarizing the general location of an individual’s movement.

track_centroid <- calc_track_centroid(
  track_data = segmented,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  trip_id_col = NULL,
  lon_col = "longitude",
  lat_col = "latitude",
  crs = 4326
)

track_centroid
## Simple feature collection with 1 feature and 2 fields
## Geometry type: POINT
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: -158.4402 ymin: 21.51123 xmax: -158.4402 ymax: 21.51123
## Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   track_id n_points             geometry
##   <chr>       <int>          <POINT [°]>
## 1 bird_001     1469 (-158.4402 21.51123)

The output is an sf object, meaning it can be mapped or used in later spatial analysis.

Calculate foraging range

The calc_foraging_range() function estimates the spatial extent of foraging points. Here, we use the phase column created earlier and calculate a convex hull around points labeled "foraging".

foraging_range <- calc_foraging_range(
  track_data = segmented,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  lon_col = "longitude",
  lat_col = "latitude",
  phase_col = "phase",
  foraging_value = "foraging",
  method = "convex_hull",
  crs = 4326
)

foraging_range
## Simple feature collection with 1 feature and 3 fields
## Geometry type: POLYGON
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: -158.8398 ymin: 21.31598 xmax: -157.8109 ymax: 21.58767
## Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
## # A tibble: 1 × 4
##   track_id n_points                                  geometry foraging_range_km2
## * <chr>       <int>                             <POLYGON [°]>              <dbl>
## 1 bird_001      178 ((-157.8112 21.31598, -157.8111 21.31601…              2441.

The output is also an sf object. It includes a geometry column and a foraging_range_km2 column, which gives the estimated range area in square kilometers.

Compare trip distance and path length

Straight-line trip distance and path length answer different movement questions. Straight-line distance asks how far the bird ended from where it started. Path length asks how much total distance the bird traveled along the observed route.

trip_metrics %>%
  select(
    track_id,
    trip_id,
    trip_distance_m,
    path_length_m,
    max_distance_from_colony_m,
    trip_duration
  )
## # A tibble: 13 × 6
##    track_id trip_id trip_distance_m path_length_m max_distance_from_colony_m
##    <chr>      <int>           <dbl>         <dbl>                      <dbl>
##  1 bird_001       1             0             0                         87.1
##  2 bird_001       2             0             0                         75.5
##  3 bird_001       3             0             0                         94.3
##  4 bird_001       4             0             0                        121. 
##  5 bird_001       5             0             0                         79.6
##  6 bird_001       6             0             0                         83.4
##  7 bird_001       7             0             0                         76.6
##  8 bird_001       8             0             0                        118. 
##  9 bird_001       9            58.5          58.5                      136. 
## 10 bird_001      10            68.2          71.2                      123. 
## 11 bird_001      11           890.       551986.                    105603. 
## 12 bird_001      12             0             0                         79.5
## 13 bird_001      13         55957.        66507.                     56147. 
## # ℹ 1 more variable: trip_duration <dbl>

In most real GPS tracks, path_length_m will be greater than or equal to trip_distance_m because birds rarely travel in a perfectly straight line.

Interpretation

The movement metrics produced in this vignette describe different aspects of seabird movement behavior. calc_trip_distance() summarizes displacement across a trip, while calc_path_length() measures the cumulative route traveled. calc_trip_duration() measures the amount of time spent away from the colony, and calc_max_distance_from_colony() identifies how far the bird traveled from its colony.

The spatial outputs, calc_track_centroid() and calc_foraging_range(), are useful for mapping and conservation analysis. These outputs can be combined with marine protected areas, fisheries effort layers, jurisdictional boundaries, or habitat data to study where birds are moving and where they may overlap with human activities.

Full workflow

The full workflow is:

gps_raw <- read.csv(
  system.file("extdata", "example_wtsh_gps.csv", package = "Shearwater"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

gps <- gps_raw %>%
  mutate(
    track_id = "bird_001",
    datetime_gmt = as.POSIXct(
      paste(Date, Time),
      format = "%m/%d/%Y %H:%M:%S",
      tz = "UTC"
    ),
    longitude = Longitude,
    latitude = Latitude
  ) %>%
  arrange(track_id, datetime_gmt)

colony_coords <- c(
  lon = gps$longitude[1],
  lat = gps$latitude[1]
)

gps <- gps %>%
  mutate(
    dist_to_colony_m = geosphere::distHaversine(
      cbind(longitude, latitude),
      matrix(
        c(colony_coords["lon"], colony_coords["lat"]),
        nrow = n(),
        ncol = 2,
        byrow = TRUE
      )
    ),
    at_colony = dist_to_colony_m <= 75
  )

segmented <- segment_trips(
  gps,
  bird_id_col = "track_id",
  datetime_col = "datetime_gmt",
  colony_flag_col = "at_colony",
  trip_id_col = "trip_id"
)

segmented <- segmented %>%
  mutate(
    phase = case_when(
      is.na(trip_id) ~ "colony",
      Speed <= median(Speed, na.rm = TRUE) ~ "foraging",
      TRUE ~ "commuting"
    )
  )

trip_distance <- calc_trip_distance(segmented)

trip_duration <- calc_trip_duration(
  segmented,
  units = "hours"
)

path_length <- calc_path_length(segmented)

max_distance <- calc_max_distance_from_colony(
  segmented,
  colony_coords = colony_coords
)

trip_metrics <- trip_distance %>%
  left_join(trip_duration, by = c("track_id", "trip_id")) %>%
  left_join(path_length, by = c("track_id", "trip_id")) %>%
  left_join(max_distance, by = c("track_id", "trip_id"))

track_centroid <- calc_track_centroid(segmented)

foraging_range <- calc_foraging_range(
  segmented,
  phase_col = "phase",
  foraging_value = "foraging"
)

Next steps

After calculating trip-level movement metrics, the next step is to summarize movement across individuals or the whole population. These outputs can then be used in later workflows for fisheries overlap, protected-area overlap, or marine spatial planning.

Developed by Cooper Owen.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
